plainMeaning: This is the ninth vow — sitting in calm equanimity (sāmāyika) — and here the reciter names and seeks pardon for the five small ways that period of stillness can go wrong: a wandering mind, careless speech, a restless body, skipping it altogether, or doing it sloppily.
recitation: Navamuṁ sāmāyika-vrata. Sāmāiya nāma sāvajja joga parivajjaṇaṁ, niravajja joga āsevaṇā ca. E navamā sāmāyika vratataṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. Maṇa duppaṇihāṇe, vaya duppaṇihāṇe, kāya duppaṇihāṇe, sāmāiyassa akaraṇayā, sāmāiyassa aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā. E pāṁca atiyāra māṁhe.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati numeral aṅka; the Gujarati ordinals ૧–૧૨ count the twelve vows.
wordByWord.notes: Structural marker, not part of the chanted Prakrit.

wordByWord.gloss: the sāmāyika vow (the vow of equanimity-sitting)
wordByWord.etymology: sāmāyika (< sama 'equanimity' + āya, the 'gaining' of evenness) + vrata 'vow'; first of the four śikṣāvrata.
wordByWord.notes: Headline naming the vow under review.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit form of Skt. sāmāyika; the -y- of sāmāyika softens to -i- in Prakrit.
Skt./Pkt. nāma 'namely, to wit', a defining particle introducing a definition.
Pkt. joga < Skt. yoga; here in its Jain technical sense of 'activity/vibration of the soul', not 'union'.

wordByWord.notes: Object of the first half: 'the giving-up of sinful activity'.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. niravajja < Skt. niravadya (nir- 'without' + avadya 'fault'); antonym of sāvajja.
Pkt. āsevaṇā < Skt. āsevanā (ā + √sev 'to serve/practise/attend upon').
wordByWord.notes: Balances parivajjaṇaṁ: shun the blameworthy, take up the blameless.
Joins the two limbs of the definition.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati demonstrative e ('this/these'), from Skt. eta-.
wordByWord.notes: Switches the register from Prakrit definition to Gujarati procedural frame.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sāmāyika (full Sanskritic spelling in the Gujarati matrix).

wordByWord.gloss: of the vow; belonging to the vow

wordByWord.etymology: Book's spelling (with ય) of atichāra < Skt. aticāra (ati + √car 'to overstep').
wordByWord.notes: Spelt atiyāra per this recension's orthography.
'I cleanse [my conscience of] the five transgressions.'

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. duppaṇihāṇa < Skt. duṣpraṇidhāna (duṣ 'ill' + praṇidhāna 'directing, fixing'); -e locative.
wordByWord.notes: First atichāra: letting the mind wander during sāmāyika.
Second atichāra: careless or improper speech during sāmāyika.
Third atichāra: restless or improper bodily posture/movement.

wordByWord.etymology: a- (privative) + karaṇa 'doing' + abstract -yā (Skt. -tā); 'the state of not-doing'.
wordByWord.notes: Fourth atichāra: failing to perform the sāmāyika at all.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. aṇavuṭṭhiya < Skt. anavasthita (an- 'not' + avasthita 'settled, established'); genitive -assa.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies an implied 'sāmāyika done in an unsettled manner'.
Fifth atichāra: performing sāmāyika carelessly / before the full time, without proper steadiness.
Seals the list: 'among these five [whatever I lapsed in...]', leading into the micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ refrain.

literalTranslation: Ninth, the sāmāyika vow. Sāmāyika, by name, is the abandoning of sinful activity, and the cultivating of blameless activity. These — I scrutinise the five transgressions of this ninth sāmāyika vow: ill-direction of mind, ill-direction of speech, ill-direction of body, the non-performance of the sāmāyika, the performing of the sāmāyika in an unsteady manner. Among these five [transgressions, whatever I have committed...].
idiomaticTranslation: The ninth vow: sāmāyika, the sitting in equanimity. What is sāmāyika? It is the laying-aside of every blameworthy activity and the taking-up only of the blameless. Now I examine the five ways this vow may have been stained: letting my mind wander, letting my tongue slip, letting my body grow restless, neglecting to sit at all, or sitting carelessly and without steadiness. Among these five — whatever fault arose, may it be undone.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Verse 9 opens the śikṣāvrata quartet, and the text pauses to embed a definition it gave no other vow: 'sāmāiya nāma sāvajja joga parivajjaṇaṁ, niravajja joga āsevaṇā ca.' This is the classical Āvaśyaka-tradition definition of sāmāyika, and its two-beat shape — shun (parivajjaṇa) the blameworthy, cultivate (āsevaṇā) the blameless — mirrors the very rhythm of pratikramaṇa itself, making this vow a miniature of the whole rite. The five atichāras then divide cleanly: the first three are the trividha-yoga of mind (maṇa), speech (vaya) and body (kāya), each spoiled by duppaṇihāṇa — 'ill-placing', the activity that leaks out where stillness was vowed. The last two move from quality to existence of the act: akaraṇayā (not doing the sāmāyika at all) and aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā (doing it unsteadily — breaking the forty-eight minutes early, or sitting without proper resolve). Note that this recension's final pair differs from the more familiar Tattvārtha list (anādara and smṛty-anupasthāna); the Anchalgachchha book substitutes the 'non-doing / unsteady-doing' couplet, a variant well-attested in Śvetāmbara āvaśyaka practice. The genitive sāmāiyassa governing both shows these last two are faults about the observance as a whole, not merely lapses within it. After the dense Prakrit definition the language reverts to the Gujarati procedural frame ('e... śodhuṁ... māṁhe'), and the verse hands off to the day/night confession refrain shared by every vow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Sāmāyika operationalises the doctrine that bondage flows from yoga (activity) driven by kaṣāya; by vowing niravadya-yoga alone for a fixed span, the householder rehearses the sarva-virati (total restraint) of the ascetic within a measured frame. The trivividha mano-vāk-kāya analysis of the first three atichāras reflects the standard Jain anatomy of action across the three channels, each capable of independent transgression. The fourth and fifth atichāras encode a subtle ethic: that a vow is wounded not only by doing it badly but by omitting it, and that half-hearted, restless observance (anavasthita) is itself a countable fault — sincerity and steadiness are part of the vow's substance, not optional extras.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter checks the day's sāmāyika against five honest questions: did my mind drift, did I speak idly, did I fidget or rise restlessly, did I skip it, did I rush or hold it half-heartedly? Naming the specific slip — rather than a vague 'I did badly' — is what the atichāra format trains, turning the next sitting into a deliberately steadier one.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.33 — five atichāras of sāmāyika (mano-/vāg-/kāya-duṣpraṇidhāna, anādara, smṛty-anupasthāna)
Āvaśyaka-sūtra / Anuyogadvāra — the definition 'sāvajja-jogaṁ pariccayāmi... sāmāiya'
Upāsakadaśāḥ-sūtra — śikṣāvrata observance by the lay disciple

sources.title: Tattvārtha-sūtra of Umāsvāti, chapter 7 (vows and their atichāras)
sources.consultedFor: The canonical five atichāras of sāmāyika and the three-fold mano-vāk-kāya duṣpraṇidhāna framework against which this recension's variant is read.

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Hindi dictionary), H. D. T. Sheth
sources.consultedFor: Glosses for duppaṇihāṇa (duṣpraṇidhāna), sāvajja/niravajja (sāvadya/niravadya), and aṇavuṭṭhiya (anavasthita).

sources.title: JainQQ digital library — Āvaśyaka and śrāvakācāra texts
sources.consultedFor: The Āvaśyaka definition of sāmāyika as sāvadya-yoga-parivarjana / niravadya-yoga-āsevana, and the 'akaraṇa / anavasthita-karaṇa' atichāra variant in Śvetāmbara practice.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit root-forms underlying the Prakrit tokens: praṇidhāna, parivarjana, āsevanā, avasthita, aticāra.

notes: Two verse-specific caveats. (1) The last two atichāras here — 'sāmāiyassa akaraṇayā' (non-performance) and 'sāmāiyassa aṇavuṭṭhiyassa karaṇayā' (unsteady performance) — diverge from the Tattvārtha-sūtra's standard pair 'anādara' (disrespect) and 'smṛty-anupasthāna' (lapse of mindfulness). This 'non-doing / unsteady-doing' couplet is a recognised Śvetāmbara āvaśyaka variant and is preserved here verbatim, not normalised to the Tattvārtha list. (2) 'aṇavuṭṭhiya' = anavasthita ('unsettled/unsteady'); a minority of readings instead derive a sense of 'doing it before the proper completion of the time-span', which is compatible with the unsteadiness gloss. The verse's per-token spellings (duppaṇihāṇe, aṇavuṭṭhiyassa) follow this book's orthography (e.g. atiyāra with ય) and were not regularised.
